meta.purpose: The single source of truth for every number KurimaSense publishes. No stat reaches a slide or caption unless it lives here with a source and year. Law 5: never fabricate stats, results, testimonials or clients.
rules: Every datapoint has: value, statement, source, year, category, confidence.
confidence: 'high' = named institution primary data; 'medium' = peer-reviewed or reputable secondary; 'context' = directional/illustrative, cite as 'estimates suggest'.
When a stat is used, the source + year MUST appear on the slide (case-study source line) or in the first comment.
Ranges are kept as ranges. Do not present '30-40%' as a single invented figure.
Re-verify any datapoint older than 3 years before headline use; drought/price figures decay fastest.

datapoints.statement: About one-fifth of cereals produced in Sub-Saharan Africa is lost between production and consumption.
datapoints.source: FAO, via World Bank 'Missing Food' analysis

datapoints.statement: As much as 37% of food produced in Sub-Saharan Africa is lost between production and consumption.
Grain post-harvest losses across Sub-Saharan Africa were valued at around US$4 billion a year — enough to meet the annual food needs of over 40 million people.
Storage and handling losses alone for cereals in Sub-Saharan Africa are estimated at 10–12%.
Across Nigeria, Ghana, South Africa, Ethiopia, Tanzania, Kenya and Uganda, average grain post-harvest loss over five years was about 15.1%.
Average smallholder maize yield in Zimbabwe is around 0.8 tonnes per hectare, against an achievable 1.4 t/ha — a productivity gap of nearly half.
datapoints.source: Frontiers in Sustainable Food Systems (Zimbabwe maize typology study)

datapoints.statement: Zimbabwe's maize yields are among the lowest in the region, where averages range from 1 to 2 tonnes per hectare.
In semi-arid areas such as Marange, maize yields range from 90 to 970 kg/ha, averaging about 355 kg/ha — a fraction of potential.
Precision technologies achieve average input savings of 8–20% and yield increases of 2–6% in systematic reviews.
datapoints.source: Agriculture (MDPI), systematic literature review of precision crop production economics

datapoints.statement: Precision nitrogen management in maize cut fertiliser use by up to 30% with no loss in yield.
datapoints.source: CIMMYT (International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center)

datapoints.statement: Variable-rate fertiliser application returned higher profits than blanket application in smallholder Sub-Saharan systems.
datapoints.source: Sustainability (MDPI), precision agriculture SSA systematic review

datapoints.statement: Advanced vegetation and stress indices can flag crop stress 10–15 days earlier than conventional visual methods.
datapoints.source: Frontiers in Plant Science (hyperspectral stress detection framework)

datapoints.statement: Vegetation indices and chlorophyll fluorescence detect water and nutrient stress before symptoms are visible to the eye.
datapoints.source: ScienceDirect, multi-dimensional optical remote sensing review

datapoints.statement: Fewer than 10% of African farmers currently have access to digital farming devices — the adoption runway is enormous.
datapoints.source: PMC review of digital agricultural service adoption (Ghana/SSA)

datapoints.statement: Over 437 digital agriculture services were tracked across the developing world, most in Sub-Saharan Africa.
Zimbabwe's tobacco export earnings hit a record US$1.3 billion in 2024, a second straight year above US$1 billion.
datapoints.source: Tobacco Industry and Marketing Board (TIMB), via Equity Axis

datapoints.statement: More than 70% of Zimbabwe's tobacco is now produced by small-scale farmers, up from a handful of large estates in 2000.
Tobacco accounts for roughly 30% of Zimbabwe's total export earnings.
Agriculture provides a livelihood for around 70% of Zimbabwe's population.
datapoints.source: Ministry of Foreign Affairs & International Trade (Zimbabwe)

datapoints.statement: Agriculture accounts for about 52.5% of total employment in Zimbabwe.
The 2023/24 El Niño drought cut Zimbabwe's maize harvest by roughly 60%, to around 635,000 tonnes from 1.5 million the season before.
Over 900,000 hectares of maize — half the 1.8 million planted — were devastated by the 2024 drought.
datapoints.source: Government of Zimbabwe (State of Disaster declaration)

datapoints.statement: More than 5.3 million Zimbabweans were projected to face food insecurity following the 2024 drought.
The 2023/24 season was Zimbabwe's worst drought in 40 years, prompting a national State of Disaster.
Zimbabwe needs about 2.2 million tonnes of cereal a year; 2024 domestic output fell far short, forcing large imports.
Of roughly 365,000 ha suitable for irrigation in Zimbabwe, under half is equipped and only about 123,000 ha is actually irrigated.
About 80% of Zimbabwe's rural population lives in Natural Regions III–V, where rainfall is erratic and dryland farming is a gamble.
datapoints.source: World Bank Climate-Smart Agriculture profile, Zimbabwe

datapoints.statement: Sub-Saharan Africa averages about 22 kg/ha of fertiliser — roughly a seventh of the world average of 146 kg/ha.
Maize is a heavy nitrogen feeder, with strong yield responses up to 150 kg N/ha — far above what most smallholders apply.
datapoints.source: PMC, fertiliser response & nitrogen use efficiency in African smallholder maize

datapoints.statement: Crop performance varies sharply within a single field — from water stress, soil differences and uneven nutrient uptake — long before the eye notices.
datapoints.source: ScienceDirect, optical remote sensing for crop stress monitoring

datapoints.statement: Sub-Saharan Africa has around 33 million smallholder farms — about 80% of all farms in the region.
Smallholders produce up to about 90% of the food supply in parts of Sub-Saharan Africa.
The median crop farm across most of Sub-Saharan Africa is between one and two hectares.
datapoints.source: FAO subnational farm-size analysis / The Lancet Planetary Health

datapoints.statement: Farms under five hectares produce around half of food calories in Sub-Saharan Africa; 5–15 ha farms add another 26%.
Around 90% of adult Zimbabweans use mobile money (led by EcoCash) — the digital rails to reach farmers already exist.
datapoints.source: University of Pretoria repository, EcoCash financial-inclusion study

datapoints.statement: About 85% of Zimbabwe's workers are informal and largely outside formal banking — mobile money is their financial system.
datapoints.source: SciELO SA, mobile money regulation & inclusion in Zimbabwe

datapoints.statement: Zimbabwe's cotton output collapsed about 96%, from ~360,000 tonnes in 2011 to ~13,000 tonnes in 2024 — climate and contract failures compounding.
Over 400,000 Zimbabwean households depend on cotton — a sector now in crisis, showing the cost of farming without resilience.
Some cotton farmers projected just three bales in 2024, against 27 the previous year — a personal-scale illustration of climate risk.
Of about 570 million farms worldwide, roughly 475 million are smallholdings under two hectares.
Solar-powered smart irrigation in Kenya (SunCulture) reported yield increases up to 300% on a fraction of conventional water — an upper-bound case, not a norm.
Nitrogen-use efficiency in Sub-Saharan maize is about a third of the world average — the same fertiliser buys far less yield when placed blind.
datapoints.source: PMC, nitrogen use efficiency in African smallholder maize

datapoints.statement: Zimbabwe has over 4 million hectares of arable land, roughly a quarter of it worked with animal or manual draught power.
datapoints.source: US Dept. of Commerce, Zimbabwe agricultural sectors guide

datapoints.statement: Only about 13,200 ha is under smallholder irrigation in Zimbabwe — the overwhelming majority of small farms are rain-dependent.
Reported return on investment for precision technologies typically ranges 5–15%, rising with how well decisions are targeted.
Farmers adopt digital tools primarily for tangible gains — higher productivity, better information, and input cost savings — not novelty.
datapoints.source: Springer, mobile agricultural service applications review (SSA)

datapoints.statement: By mid-2025 Zimbabwe had sold over 306 million kg of tobacco for about US$1.03 billion, at an average US$3.36/kg — a rebound from the drought year.
Maize typically needs on the order of 100 kg of nitrogen per hectare for good yields — several times the regional average application.
datapoints.source: PMC, fertiliser response in African smallholder maize

datapoints.statement: Perishable fruit and vegetables suffer far higher post-harvest losses than cereals, concentrating risk in horticulture value chains.
datapoints.source: PMC review, food loss and waste in Sub-Saharan Africa

datapoints.statement: Southern Africa's maize belt is exposed to a recurring El Niño drought cycle — the 2024 event was severe but not isolated, making resilience a standing requirement.
A national South African survey shows precision-agriculture adoption climbing among commercial grain farmers — the technology curve nearest to Zimbabwe's commercial sector.
datapoints.source: SA Grain magazine, national precision-ag survey

datapoints.statement: NDVI combined with deep learning has reached crop-health classification accuracy near 98% in recent studies — the analytical layer behind a reliable field score.
datapoints.source: arXiv / ResearchGate, NDVI + neural network crop health classification
